original_abstract: Architected metamaterials offer the potential to achieve extraordinary mechanical properties by tailoring their spatial configurations. However, traditional inverse design methods struggle to replicate the full complexity of mechanical responses, such as non-linear stress-strain curves, due to the challenges of multi-objective optimization and inherent manufacturing defects. In this work, we present a rapid inverse design framework that utilizes generative machine learning and desktop additive manufacturing to create metamaterials based on user-prescribed uniaxial stress-strain curves. By integrating a surrogate forward neural network with a generative inverse model, our methodology effectively maps complex target behaviors to optimal topological parameters while explicitly accounting for process-dependent fabrication errors. We demonstrate that this approach can replicate a vast array of mechanical footprints, including monotonic and cyclic behaviors, with approximately 90% fidelity between target and experimental results. Furthermore, we illustrate the practical utility of this framework by designing an architected shoe midsole with spatially tailored propulsion and cushioning, as well as compound lattices with programmed, multi-step energy absorption. By bypassing conventional iterative design cycles, this methodology provides a robust pathway for the rapid realization of materials with fully customizable, non-linear mechanical functionalities.
humanized_abstract: Using the concept of architected metamaterials to engineer extraordinary mechanical behaviour by designing the right structure has become increasingly prevalent in scientific literature. However, inversely designing such architectures to accurately replicate and surpass the complexity of their native mechanical response—especially nonlinear stress–strain relations—is highly challenging due to the multi-objective nature of the optimisation process, coupled with inherent manufacturing anomalies. In this article, we introduce an inverse design approach based on generative machine learning (ML) and 3D printing additive manufacturing, capable of designing and 3D printing architected metamaterials using a prespecified uniaxial stress–strain relationship as input. Our strategy leverages a combination of a surrogate forward neural network model (simulating the actual properties of the designed material based on topological descriptors) and a conditional generative adversarial network-based (GAN) inverse model for the prompt translation of desired stress–strain profiles to the optimal architecture required. Through this innovative method, we obtain a generalisation of a large range of mechanical paths—including monotonic and cyclic stress–strain relations—that can replicate a desired material response with 90% average accuracy with respect to experimental validation, thereby establishing a flexible framework for the material-by-design fabrication of functional architected metamaterials. Moreover, this method opens new avenues for 3D printing advanced and optimised sole architectures such as bespoke shoe midsoles tailored with individualised propulsion and cushioning at each location along the sole length, in addition to compound lattice materials that can provide controlled and programmable multi-stage energy dissipation characteristics. Moreover, the ability to fabricate such tailored metamaterials within few iterations lessens the extensive trial and error approach associated with material design, hence facilitating an expedited pathway for transforming bespoke stress–strain behaviour into designed materials functionalities at the macro-scale.
